text: Hi, I'm Dan Resnicek from Pacific Northwest Urology. And today we're going to be comparing three different robotic partial nephrectomies. These are all the same procedure, but they're done on different masses. They're different sizes and different locations. Some are much more easy than one another. And I think these are great examples to show you the variations in this procedure. So we'll start off with a big tumor. We'll look at one that's central right next to the renal artery. And finally, we'll look at a small tumor on an off clamp robotic partial nephrectomy.

The surgeon clamps the renal artery to stop blood flow to the left kidney, which has been flipped 180 degrees for resection. Using cold scissors, the surgeon carefully dissects the large exophytic tumor from the kidney, revealing its capsule and separating it from the surrounding tissue. The tumor is removed, and the base is fulgurated to ensure hemostasis. Sutures are placed in the deep layer of the kidney to repair the defect, using plastic bolsters to bring the edges together and minimize bleeding.

The surgeon prepares to clamp the renal artery, which is visible in front of a large central kidney mass. The spleen is visible in the background. The tumor is adjacent to the renal artery, located centrally within the kidney. The surgeon clamps the artery and begins cutting into the kidney capsule, revealing the tumor capsule beneath. An arterial bleed is observed, and a clip is applied to the vessel supplying the tumor. The tumor is dissected and peeled away from the kidney, preserving normal kidney tissue. The tumor is placed in a bag to prevent spillage, and a hemostatic bolster is sutured in place to aid in repair.

The surgeon addresses a small superior tumor on the top of the kidney, measuring approximately 1.5 centimeters. The tumor is resected without clamping the renal artery, allowing continuous blood flow to the kidney. Minimal bleeding is observed during the resection. The repair involves two sutures and the application of Surgicel to minimize postoperative bleeding. The kidney is repositioned under the liver, with previously removed fat placed back in position.

And that's it. So to review, we compare different partial nephrectomies today, all done robotically. We had three different cases. We had tumors in different locations. We had exophytic tumors, endophytic tumors, and a central tumor and different sizes. We had one very small mass that could be removed off clamp rather easily. We had one large mass, almost the same size as the kidney, but because of its location was easy to remove. And last, we had one central mass right next to the renal artery and vein. However, because it had a great capsule, it was able to be removed while sparing the kidney. So hopefully you guys enjoyed this video today. I'm again, Dan Resnicek from Pacific Northwest Urology. If you liked the video, please click like below or subscribe to our channel. Thanks again.
